title: China Targets Japan with Export Controls, Ukraine Burns Russian Refineries, Fighter Jet Alliances Reshuffle
subhead: Beijing blacklists 20 Japanese entities over remilitarization concerns, Ukraine escalates drone strikes on Russian energy infrastructure, Germany pivots from FCAS to F-35s and GCAP, and the US races to lock down critical mineral supply chains before a 2027 deadline.

sections.heading: China Adds 20 Japanese Entities to Export Control List
sections.body:
China's Ministry of Commerce added 20 Japanese organizations and companies to its export control list on June 29, restricting dual-use technology exports to entities it says are involved in enhancing Japan's military capabilities. Another 20 Japanese entities were placed on a separate watch list, according to the South China Morning Post.

The targeted entities include Japan's National Institute for Defense Studies and military research institutes for ground, naval, and air weapons systems, as well as several subsidiaries under the Mitsubishi Electric and Mitsubishi Heavy Industries umbrella, spanning defense systems, space, precision instruments, and maritime technology. The list also includes Fujitsu, Komatsu, Terra Drone Corp, ACSL Ltd, and OKI Electric Industry, per the EconoTimes.

Beijing said the move aims to deter Japan's 'remilitarisation' and prevent Japan from 'acquiring nuclear weapons.' A ministry spokesperson claimed the measures target only a 'small number' of entities and 'will not affect normal economic and trade exchanges.' The controls require Chinese exporters to obtain government approval before supplying the listed entities with dual-use goods and technologies, and to provide assurances that products will not strengthen Japan's military capabilities.

The escalation follows a months-long deterioration in Sino-Japanese ties triggered by Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's comments about potential military intervention in Taiwan. At the G7 summit, Takaichi pushed allies for a coordinated response to China's trade tactics, the New York Times reported, signaling rising concern over what she termed 'China Shock 2.0.'
sections.whyItMatters: China is weaponizing export controls against Japan with unprecedented specificity — targeting defense research institutes and major industrial conglomerates by name. The move comes as Japan deepens security cooperation with South Korea and seeks GCAP partners, and signals Beijing's willingness to escalate economic coercion in response to strategic alignment it perceives as threatening.

sections.heading: Ukraine Drone Strikes Force Putin to Acknowledge Fuel Deficit
sections.body:
Ukraine markedly stepped up its long-range drone campaign against Russian energy infrastructure this week, setting fire to the Slavyansk-na-Kubani refinery in Krasnodar Krai — one of southern Russia's major refineries, processing close to 4 million tons of crude per year — and striking a second refinery in the Yaroslavl region, roughly 700 km from the Ukrainian border, the AP reported.

President Zelenskyy described the strikes as 'long-range sanctions,' stating: 'Each strike means a reduction in the resources that fuel the Russian war machine, and another step toward peace.' Ukraine has also been choking off fuel deliveries to Crimea, where Kremlin-installed officials suspended gasoline sales to civilians after supply routes were targeted.

For the first time, President Putin publicly acknowledged Russia faces a 'certain deficit' of fuel, vowing to strengthen protection of oil facilities and boost output. The admission came as fuel rationing spread across Russian regions and long lines formed at gas stations. Putin framed Ukraine's campaign as an attempt to 'cause a split in Russian society,' but insisted the strikes 'have absolutely no effect on the situation at the front.'

Meanwhile, StrategyPage reports that Russia has launched approximately 60,000 Shahed/Geran drones at Ukraine over four years, and that Ukraine's layered anti-drone system — combining wide-area radio jamming, interceptor drones, and ground-based automatic gun teams — now achieves roughly 90% interception rates during mass attacks. Ukraine has manufactured over 5 million drones since late 2023.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine is proving that inexpensive drone warfare can impose strategic costs on a larger adversary's economy and logistics. Putin's public acknowledgment of fuel shortages — a first — shows the campaign is biting. The broader lesson, per StrategyPage, is that European NATO states are unprepared for drone warfare at scale and are now scrambling to replicate Ukraine's affordable layered defense model.

sections.heading: Germany Pivots from FCAS to F-35s and GCAP as Fighter Landscape Reshuffles
sections.body:
Germany officially terminated the Future Combat Air System (FCAS) 6th-generation fighter program with France in June 2026, after years of deadlock in which Dassault reportedly demanded 80% workshare, reducing Germany's Airbus to a supporting role. The collapse mirrors France's earlier departure from the 1980s Eurofighter program to build the Rafale independently.

Germany is now pursuing multiple paths: ordering 20 additional Eurofighter Typhoon Tranche 5 jets (delivery 2031-2034), considering 15 more F-35As beyond its initial 35, and urgently developing 'Jagdbomberdrohne' (fighter-bomber) combat drones, Simple Flying reports. For a 6th-generation fighter, Germany's options are: joining the GCAP/Tempest program (UK-Italy-Japan) as a partner, leading a new program with Sweden and/or Spain, or relying on F-35s operating alongside autonomous aircraft into the 2050s without a European next-gen fighter.

The GCAP program itself is attracting new interest. Canadian Defence Minister David McGuinty discussed the program with his Japanese counterpart on June 25, calling it a 'promising initiative,' Reuters reported. Italy says more nations are willing to join. Meanwhile, Leonardo's new CEO warned that Germany's potential entry would need to happen quickly given the program's timeline — a 2035 entry-into-service target — and that advantages of a new partner include 'extra cash and know-how,' but timing is critical. The UK eyes a GCAP contract signing within weeks, Breaking Defense reports.
sections.whyItMatters: The collapse of FCAS removes the last major Franco-German defense industrial partnership and fragments European fighter development into two competing programs (GCAP and whatever France builds alone). Canada's interest in GCAP signals widening appeal, but Germany's potential entry would reshape the program's cost structure and industrial base — and the window for joining is narrow.

sections.heading: US Races to Secure Tungsten Supply Before 2027 Procurement Deadline
sections.body:
The US defense industry is scrambling to establish domestic tungsten supply chains ahead of a federal procurement rule taking effect January 1, 2027 that bars tungsten sourced from China and other designated countries from a wide range of defense applications. China controls approximately 80% of global mined tungsten supply and much of the refining chain, Interesting Engineering reports.

Rotterdam APT (ammonium paratungstate), the main tungsten benchmark, has surged roughly 900% from 2025 lows and 350% year-to-date, now trading at $3,000-$3,200 per metric tonne unit. In late 2025, China restricted tungsten exports to only 15 approved companies for 2026-27, tightening control over volumes and destinations.

On the domestic front, Western Star has deployed field crews to its White Star Tungsten Project in Elko County, Nevada — a past-producing site at the former Mission Cross Mine — launching the first modern exploration program including drone-based magnetic surveys. Separately, the Pentagon selected Titan Mining and REalloys to build critical mineral processing facilities on US military bases. Titan Mining's subsidiary Empire State Mines received conditional leases at Pine Bluff Arsenal, Arkansas and Anniston Army Depot, Alabama for graphite processing — the first-ever public-private partnership for domestic critical minerals processing on strategic defense installations, Reuters reported on June 25.

The supply squeeze has also drawn political scrutiny. A New York Times investigation revealed that the $1.6 billion US-Kazakhstan tungsten mining deal involves companies tied to the Trump and Lutnick families. The deal, signed November 6 without disclosing those connections, designates Kaz Resources as the operator — a company linked to Eric Trump, Donald Trump Jr., and Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick's sons Brandon and Kyle. At least 14 companies affiliated with one or both families are working with the federal government on critical mining deals, Mother Jones reported.
sections.whyItMatters: The 2027 procurement ban is a hard deadline that will force defense contractors to restructure supply chains or lose eligibility for Pentagon contracts. With no operating domestic tungsten mine and China tightening export controls, the US faces a genuine strategic vulnerability — and the Trump family's involvement in the Kazakhstan deal raises conflict-of-interest questions at the intersection of national security and private profit.

sections.heading: US Plans Permanent Weapons Stockpile in Australia; UK Seizes Russian Shadow Fleet Tanker
sections.body:
US Navy tender documents reveal advanced planning for a permanent war-ready weapons stockpile for the Marine Corps in Victoria, Australia, with $30 million allocated to build warehouses and offices at the Australian military base in Bandiana. The stockpile — the first land-based Marine Corps pre-positioning in Australia — will store munitions, vehicles, and equipment to sustain thousands of troops, SCMP reports, citing AFP. The facility is expected to reach full capacity by 2028 and will be located beyond the range of most Chinese missiles.

The move follows the establishment of a similar Marine Corps stockpile in the Philippines — the first in the Asia-Pacific — expected to open this year. Together, the two sites represent a significant expansion of US pre-positioning posture aimed at deterring China and enabling rapid deployment in a Taiwan contingency.

Meanwhile in Europe, British Royal Marines seized the Russian shadow fleet tanker Smyrtos in the English Channel — the first such seizure of a Russian vessel by UK forces. The ship's captain, Indian national Ajay Pant, was charged with contravening Russia sanctions. The UK simultaneously announced a new sanctions package targeting Russia's shadow oil fleet and finance networks at the G7 summit. Canada followed with its own sanctions on Russia's shadow fleet, energy revenues, and defense-industrial sector. The seizures mark an escalation from financial sanctions to physical enforcement, and Russia responded by having a warship fire warning shots at a British yacht in the English Channel days later.
sections.whyItMatters: The Australian stockpile extends US Marine pre-positioning deeper into the Pacific, creating a southern axis of deterrence that complicates Chinese military planning. In Europe, the UK's physical seizure of a shadow fleet tanker — and Russia's tit-for-tat warning shots — marks a shift from sanctioning Russian oil revenue in the abstract to intercepting it at sea.

sections.heading: Japan and South Korea Deepen Defense Cooperation; Poland Warns of Russian Incursion Risk
sections.body:
Japanese Defense Minister Shinjiro Koizumi and South Korean counterpart Ahn Gyu-back held their fourth face-to-face meeting in Seoul on June 28, agreeing to advance talks on defense equipment and technology cooperation following Tokyo's April revision of its defense equipment transfer rules, Nikkei Asia reported.

The ministers welcomed the resumption of joint search-and-rescue exercises between the Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force and South Korean Navy — the first in about nine years — and the launch of bilateral talks on artificial intelligence in defense. They also agreed to broaden drills for maritime emergency response and expand exchanges between Japan's Blue Impulse and South Korea's Black Eagles aerobatic teams. It was only the second time Japanese and South Korean defense ministers have exchanged visits within a single year, and the first in 23 years.

Koizumi condemned the previous day's joint flight by Chinese and Russian military aircraft over the Sea of Japan and East China Sea, saying it 'could only be viewed as a show of force directed at our country.' The ministers reaffirmed trilateral coordination with the US on North Korean denuclearization.

Separately, Poland's intelligence chief Colonel Pawel Szota warned of a growing risk of Russian provocations along NATO's eastern flank, citing the possibility of 'little green men' operations against the Baltic states. 'Russia is systematically crossing red lines to test NATO's response,' Szota told Rzeczpospolita. 'The costs of such provocations are low, while the alliance responds mainly with political measures, which invites further escalation.' Polish Foreign Minister Radoslaw Sikorski warned of possible Russian false-flag operations, DPA reported.
sections.whyItMatters: The Japan-South Korea defense normalization is accelerating — from suspended exercises to active cooperation on AI, equipment transfers, and joint drills within a single year. The timing, coinciding with China's export controls on Japanese entities and the China-Russia joint patrol, underscores that Northeast Asian security architecture is shifting from bilateral US alliances toward trilateral and minilateral frameworks. Meanwhile, Poland's warning highlights a persistent NATO vulnerability: low-cost Russian provocations that the alliance struggles to deter proportionally.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Fire Point Targets Domestic Ballistic Missile Interceptor; Europe Eyes Affordable Air Defense
sections.body:
Ukrainian defense company Fire Point — maker of the Flamingo cruise missile — is accelerating plans to develop a domestic ballistic missile interceptor, with flight tests for its FP-9 ballistic missile expected over the summer and first interceptors potentially ready by year-end, Reuters reported on June 25. Fire Point has signed an agreement with German radar maker Hensoldt to integrate European radar technology into the system.

President Zelensky also said Ukraine is requesting US approval to manufacture anti-ballistic missile systems domestically, the Kyiv Post reported — a significant shift that would transfer production capability rather than just supply interceptors.

The push comes as European nations reassess their own air defense posture. StrategyPage reported that high-end systems like Patriot and SAMP/T are unavailable, unaffordable, and unsuited to countering mass drone barrages. Russia has launched roughly 60,000 Shahed/Geran drones at Ukraine in four years. Ukraine's layered defense — wide-area jamming, interceptor drones, and ground-based automatic gun teams — achieves 90% interception rates at a fraction of the cost of guided missiles. Gulf states have contracted with Ukraine to purchase and set up anti-drone defenses, StrategyPage reports, and European nations are seeking to replicate the model.
sections.whyItMatters: If Fire Point succeeds, Ukraine would move from importing air defense systems to producing its own — and potentially exporting them. The Hensoldt partnership signals a European-Ukrainian industrial convergence on missile defense. For NATO, the deeper lesson is that affordable, layered drone defense outperforms expensive guided missiles at scale — a doctrinal shift with implications for every European defense budget.

bottomLine: Three interconnected shifts define this week in defense: China is escalating economic coercion against Japan at the same moment Japan and South Korea are deepening defense ties — a Northeast Asian security realignment that cuts across old grievances. In Europe, the UK's seizure of a Russian shadow fleet tanker and Russia's warning-shot response mark the transition from financial sanctions to physical confrontation at sea. And across both theaters, the underlying pattern is the same: affordable, scalable systems — drones, layered air defense, pre-positioned stockpiles, and mineral supply chains — are mattering more than legacy platforms. The B-52 crash at Edwards AFB that killed all eight crew, a reminder that even the oldest airframes in the US fleet carry human risk, underscores the urgency of the modernization decisions now being made from Berlin to Canberra.
sources: South China Morning Post, EconoTimes, New York Times, Associated Press, StrategyPage, Simple Flying, Reuters, Nikkei Asia, Yahoo News/DPA, Interesting Engineering, Mother Jones, Kyiv Post, Kyiv Independent, Breaking Defense, Japan Times, SCMP/AFP, The Guardian, CNBC
